title: The Fascinating Engineering of the Titanic: How the Great Ocean Liner Was Built
description:
in History, Technology |   	March 16th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When many of us first learned of the RMS Titan┬Łic, it was pre┬Łsent┬Łed first
body:
in History, Technology |   	March 16th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When many of us first learned of the RMS Titan┬Łic, it was pre┬Łsent┬Łed first as one of his┬Łto┬ŁryŌĆÖs great┬Łest ironies: the ŌĆ£unsink┬ŁableŌĆØ ocean lin┬Łer that went down on its maid┬Łen voy┬Łage. Of course, thereŌĆÖs a great deal more to the sto┬Łry, as any┬Łone who becomes obsessed with the ill-fat┬Łed ship┬Ā(James Cameron being just one notable exam┬Łple) under┬Łstands full well. Even apart from the many human expe┬Łri┬Łences sur┬Łround┬Łing it,┬Āsome of them told by the wreck┬ŁŌĆÖs sur┬Łvivors and pre┬Łserved on film, the mechan┬Łi┬Łcal aspects of the┬ĀTitan┬Łic hold out con┬Łsid┬Łer┬Łable fas┬Łci┬Łna┬Łtion for any┬Łone with an engi┬ŁneerŌĆÖs cast of mind. Put aside, for the moment, the mat┬Łter of the sink┬Łing, and con┬Łsid┬Łer just what went into mak┬Łing it one of the most glo┬Łri┬Łous cre┬Łations of man launched into the ocean to date ŌĆö or rather, one of the three most glo┬Łri┬Łous.
The Titan┬Łic was one of a trio of sim┬Łi┬Łlar White Star Line ships com┬Łplet┬Łed in the ear┬Łly nine┬Łteen-tens. In the video above, Bill Ham┬Łmack, known on YouTube as Engi┬Łneer┬Łguy, tells the sto┬Łry of not just the┬ĀTitan┬Łic, but also the┬ĀOlympic and the HMHS Bri┬Łtan┬Łnic. An engi┬Łneer┬Łing pro┬Łfes┬Łsor at the Uni┬Łver┬Łsi┬Łty of Illi┬Łnois, he found in the cam┬Łpus library issues of the jour┬Łnal┬ĀThe Engi┬Łneer pub┬Łlished between 1909 and 1911 that con┬Łtain detailed pho┬Łtographs of the con┬Łstruc┬Łtion of both┬Āthe┬ĀTitan┬Łic and┬ĀOlympic, sis┬Łter ships that were built side-by-side.
One ele┬Łment high┬Łlight┬Łed that we may not much con┬Łsid┬Łer today is the sheer scale of the things: each was held togeth┬Łer by three mil┬Łlion riv┬Łets, could con┬Łtain 1.5 mil┬Łlion gal┬Łlons of bal┬Łlast water, weighed 52,000 tons when ful┬Łly fit┬Łted, required 23 tons of lubri┬Łcant to slide from the dock into the water, and burned┬Ā650 tons of┬Ācoal per day on a transat┬Łlantic cross┬Łing.
Alas, size alone was┬ŁnŌĆÖt enough to pre┬Łvent dis┬Łas┬Łter. ŌĆ£Less than a year after the launch of these two giant ships, one suf┬Łfered a col┬Łli┬Łsion that ripped a gap┬Łing hole in its side,ŌĆØ says Ham┬Łmack. ŌĆ£That ship was of course, the Olympic.ŌĆØ Its sud┬Łden encounter with a pass┬Łing war┬Łship neces┬Łsi┬Łtat┬Łed patch┬Łing with wood before it could return home for a full repair, but there┬Łafter it remained in ser┬Łvice for near┬Łly a quar┬Łter-cen┬Łtu┬Łry. Its less lucky sib┬Łling┬Āend┬Łed up at the bot┬Łtom of the ocean after run┬Łning into trou┬Łble of its own: a mine and a tor┬Łpe┬Łdo spelled the end for┬Āthe Bri┬Łtan┬Łnic┬Āin 1916. As for the┬ĀTitan┬Łic, we all know about its fate┬Łful encounter with the ice┬Łberg, and maybe weŌĆÖve even heard dis┬Łcus┬Łsions of how its design┬Łers could have mit┬Łi┬Łgat┬Łed the impact: more or taller bulk┬Łheads, a dou┬Łble hull rather than just a dou┬Łble bot┬Łtom, greater lifeboat capac┬Łi┬Łty. As for whether and how those solu┬Łtions would have worked, per┬Łhaps Ham┬Łmack could still shoot a fol┬Łlow-up explain┬Łing it all to us.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
The First Full 3D Scan of the Titan┬Łic, Made of More Than 700,000 Images Cap┬Łtur┬Łing the WreckŌĆÖs Every Detail
See the First 8K Footage of the Titan┬Łic, the High┬Łest-Qual┬Łi┬Łty Video of the Ship┬Łwreck Yet
The Titan┬Łic: Rare Footage of the Ship Before Dis┬Łas┬Łter Strikes (1911ŌĆō1912)
Titan┬Łic Sur┬Łvivor Inter┬Łviews: What It Was Like to Flee the Sink┬Łing Lux┬Łu┬Łry Lin┬Łer
The Sink┬Łing of the Bri┬Łtan┬Łnic: An Ani┬Łmat┬Łed Intro┬Łduc┬Łtion to the Titan┬ŁicŌĆÖs For┬Łgot┬Łten Sis┬Łter Ship
How a 16th-Cen┬Łtu┬Łry ExplorerŌĆÖs Sail┬Łing Ship Worked: An Ani┬Łmat┬Łed Video Takes You on a Com┬Łpre┬Łhen┬Łsive Tour
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: How to Read Books That Challenge Your Mind: Advice from Robert Greene, Author of The 48 Laws of Power
description: If youŌĆÖve fall┬Łen out of the habit of read┬Łing books, youŌĆÖre cer┬Łtain┬Łly not alone. Con┬Łsid┬Łer how often posts cir┬Łcu┬Łlate on social media (i
body:
If youŌĆÖve fall┬Łen out of the habit of read┬Łing books, youŌĆÖre cer┬Łtain┬Łly not alone. Con┬Łsid┬Łer how often posts cir┬Łcu┬Łlate on social media (itself a big part of the prob┬Łlem) about stud┬Łies show┬Łing a rapid increase in the num┬Łber of peo┬Łple who donŌĆÖt even get one book read per year. How best to get back on the lit┬Łer┬Łary wag┬Łon? You might try going straight for the hard stuff, as it were, by tak┬Łing on a nov┬Łel like┬ĀMoby-Dick. But that, accord┬Łing to the view artic┬Łu┬Łlat┬Łed in the video above by┬ĀThe 48 Laws of Pow┬Łer author Robert Greene, would be like step┬Łping back into the gym after years away and try┬Łing to bench press 300 pounds. Rather than start┬Łing with MelvilleŌĆÖs mas┬Łter┬Łpiece, build your way up to it ŌĆö but once you get to it, youŌĆÖve got to fin┬Łish it.
ŌĆ£You want to train your┬Łself to fin┬Łish books, and not con┬Łstant┬Łly be going from one to anoth┬Łer to anoth┬Łer,ŌĆØ Greene says, and that holds even for those you may not enjoy. ŌĆ£When I read a book that I hate, that is bor┬Łing, and I make myself read all the way through, I kind of take angry notes about it: God, this is ridicu┬Łlous, this is so stu┬Łpid, I hate this, this guy does┬ŁnŌĆÖt know what heŌĆÖs talk┬Łing about. You can react to the book, you can have a dia┬Łlogue with it, but you want to be able to have the patience to get through a 400- 500, 600-page book.ŌĆØ To return to the weight-train┬Łing anal┬Ło┬Łgy, a short┬Łer book to start could be ŌĆ£kind of easy, and it could be in a sub┬Łject that inter┬Łests you,ŌĆØ but upon fin┬Łish┬Łing it, you should ŌĆ£choose some┬Łthing thatŌĆÖs a lit┬Łtle bit dif┬Łfi┬Łcult and a lit┬Łtle bit com┬Łpli┬Łcat┬Łed.ŌĆØ
The idea is delib┬Łer┬Łate┬Łly to choose books that chal┬Łlenge you, a qual┬Łi┬Łty that does┬ŁnŌĆÖt come from length alone. If one gives you an impres┬Łsion that ŌĆ£itŌĆÖs not how you feel polit┬Łi┬Łcal┬Łly about the world, itŌĆÖs not how you feel spir┬Łi┬Łtu┬Łal┬Łly about the world, itŌĆÖs some┬Łthing thatŌĆÖs a lit┬Łtle bit out┬Łside what you would nor┬Łmal┬Łly encounter,ŌĆØ take it as a sign that you should read it. Approach┬Łing the mat┬Łter from the oth┬Łer direc┬Łtion, Greene also advis┬Łes not to ŌĆ£just choose things that you think are enter┬Łtain┬Łing and fun, because thatŌĆÖs going to make you lazy, and itŌĆÖs going to make you weak, and itŌĆÖs going to make you always look for things that are enter┬Łtain┬Łing, fun, and dis┬Łtract┬Łing.ŌĆØ An air┬Łport-thriller-heavy diet may work for Mal┬Łcolm Glad┬Łwell, but itŌĆÖs unlike┬Łly to work for you.
As an exam┬Łple of a chal┬Łleng┬Łing read, Greene points to any of Robert CaroŌĆÖs four bio┬Łgraph┬Łi┬Łcal vol┬Łumes about U.S. Pres┬Łi┬Łdent Lyn┬Łdon B. John┬Łson, one of which runs to well over 1,000 pages. He could also have sug┬Łgest┬Łed CaroŌĆÖs ear┬Łli┬Łer┬ĀThe Pow┬Łer Bro┬Łker: Robert Moses and the Fall of New York, one of those books ŌĆö along┬Łside not just┬ĀMoby-Dick, but also the Bible,┬ĀUlysses,┬ĀWar and Peace,┬Āand even Pride and Prej┬Łu┬Łdice┬ĀŌĆö┬Āthat even many seri┬Łous read┬Łers only pre┬Łtend to have made it through, or indeed start┬Łed. What┬Łev┬Łer the mate┬Łr┬Łi┬Łal you use, Greene says,┬ĀŌĆ£I beg you to train your mind like you train your body, to go through some┬Łthing thatŌĆÖs a lit┬Łtle more dif┬Łfi┬Łcult, a lit┬Łtle more chal┬Łleng┬Łing, thatŌĆÖs going to take some time for you. ThatŌĆÖs the way to devel┬Łop the habit of want┬Łi┬Łng to read, and to have the patience to read more books.ŌĆØ No pain, no gain, as the body┬Łbuilders say; bear that in mind when you get to MelvilleŌĆÖs dis┬Łtilled course on cetol┬Łogy.
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
How to Read Five Books Per Month & Become a Seri┬Łous Read┬Łer: Tips from Deep Work Author Cal New┬Łport
20 Books Peo┬Łple Pre┬Łtend to Read (and Now Your Con┬Łfes┬Łsions?)
How to Read Many More Books in a Year: Watch a Short Doc┬Łu┬Łmen┬Łtary Fea┬Łtur┬Łing Some of the WorldŌĆÖs Most Beau┬Łti┬Łful Book┬Łstores
5 Books You Can Read Again .ŌĆ” and Again and Again: HereŌĆÖs Our Picks, Now Yours
7 Tips for Read┬Łing More Books in a Year
The Nature of Human Stu┬Łpid┬Łi┬Łty Explained by The 48 Laws of Pow┬Łer Author Robert Greene
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Extreme Macro Photos of Insect Wings by Chris Perani Layer Thousands of Images
description: For Chris Perani, the most diminutive detailsŌĆöthe kind that are virtually impossible to see with the naked eyeŌĆöare an endless source of wonder. Hi
body:
For Chris Perani, the most diminutive detailsŌĆöthe kind that are virtually impossible to see with the naked eyeŌĆöare an endless source of wonder. His ongoing series, Wings, focuses on the prismatic effect of insectsŌĆÖ anatomy in what he describes as ŌĆ£extreme macro.ŌĆØ The images reveal details weŌĆÖd otherwise only be able to see clearly beneath a microscope, and a meticulous process illuminates undulating, scaled surfaces that resemble chromatic pixels, stained glass, or even beadwork.
Perani uses special lenses that magnify objects up to 10 times, but he also takes up to 2,000 carefully measured shots of each specimen. He then digitally stacks them to achieve incredible clarity and dimension. Each high-resolution image is captured at 10-micron intervalsŌĆöa distance shorter than the width of a human hairŌĆöso precision is paramount.
Bee
The organic architecture of beesŌĆÖ wings, plus those of wasps, damselflies, beetles, and butterflies, illustrates the precision of their anatomy and the way a prism of hues is produced by both pigmentation and structural color, like iridescence.
ŌĆ£With many of these insects, light completely changes the result,ŌĆØ Perani says. ŌĆ£Bees, for example, often have wings that appear dark and colorless at first glance. But when light hits them at exactly the right angle, thin-film interference suddenly reveals remarkable colors, textures, and intricate structures across the wingŌĆÖs surface, turning what first appears dark into a delicate fabric of light and structure.ŌĆØ
See more on PeraniŌĆÖs Instagram.
Male Valanga nigricornis
Madagascan sunset moth
Jeweled flower mantis
Papilio ulysses

Xylocopa latipes
Damselfly

title: White Lace Proliferates Across Urban Spaces in Patterned Murals by NeSpoon
description: NeSpoon continues to embellish the sides residential complexes and facades of historic buildings around the world with her striking murals. Taking
body:
NeSpoon continues to embellish the sides residential complexes and facades of historic buildings around the world with her striking murals. Taking the deceptively simple concept of lace and translating it to an architectonic scale, the artist adds a striking element of whimsy and a nod to craft that peek out around city corners.
As far as we can tell, lace has been around since at least the early 16th century, and it may have originated in Venice. Over the centuries, as the practice evolved and its popularity grew, so did its styles and the techniques used to create it. ThereŌĆÖs bobbin lace, which involves fiber attached to small bobbins, which are used to braid or twist the thread into a delicate fabric. And then thereŌĆÖs needlelace, which evolved from a number of other kinds of stitched techniques.

NeSpoon uses paint and the power of contrast to create large-scale lace patterns in a celebration of the craft. Often symmetrical, they appear framed by the outlines of corners and roofs, while windows and doors emphasize the muralsŌĆÖ scale. From a distance, the patterns appear flawless, as if they could be printed. Up close, itŌĆÖs clear the lines are sprayed and brushed by hand, emphasizing the handmade.
Among several art festivals this year, NeSpoon is headed to Valence, France, in May to transform several connected buildings across three blocks. And she is among 100 artists invited to create a work for the reopening of the Pergamonmuseum in Berlin to mark the reopening of the institution in 2027 after an extended renovation. ŌĆ£It means a lot to me, as the Pergamonmuseum was the first museum I ever visited as a child,ŌĆØ the artist says.
See more on NeSpoonŌĆÖs Instagram and YouTube channel. You may also enjoy the ancient art of kumihomo, a traditional Japanese braiding technique that uses bobbins.

schema.headline: White Lace Proliferates Across Urban Spaces in Patterned Murals by NeSpoon

title: Thomas GainsboroughŌĆÖs Portraits of Pride and Prejudice
description:
Art Review
                The English artistŌĆÖs paintings work hard to make social hierarchy feel beautiful, even natural.
body:
Art Review
                The English artistŌĆÖs paintings work hard to make social hierarchy feel beautiful, even natural. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Thomas Gainsborough, "Mr. and Mrs. Andrews" (c. 1750), oil on canvas (┬® The National Gallery, London; all photos courtesy the Frick Collection)
    
        
            The first room of Gainsborough: The Fashion of Portraiture opens on a genre already falling out of favor. The ŌĆ£conversation piecesŌĆØ gathered here were considered provincial by the London elite by the time Gainsborough painted them. Working from the Suffolk countryside and trained partly in the archaic conventions of English painter Francis Hayman, he gives ŌĆ£The Gravenor FamilyŌĆØ (1754) a similarly formal stiffness, arranging their figures with a peculiar sameness. Yet ŌĆ£Mr. and Mrs. AndrewsŌĆØ (1750) demonstrates what Gainsborough could already do that his contemporaries could not: make landscape and clothing equally legible as declarations of ownership and belonging, devoting as much attention to the bountiful farmland as Frances AndrewsŌĆÖs delicate dress folds and pink satin mules. When Gainsborough moved to Bath in 1759, he refined his style in sustained dialogue with Flemish painter Anthony van Dyck, translating his dramatically lit garments and aristocratic ease into the terms of Georgian self-fashioning.Thomas Gainsborough, "The Gravenor Family" (c. 1754), oil on canvasAttitudes toward British portraiture have soured in recent decades, a problem curator Aimee Ng named plainly at the exhibition's press preview. These paintings have long read as tired images of the wealthy who benefited from colonization and the labor of enslaved people. The exhibition acknowledges this, while insisting that more is enmeshed in GainsboroughŌĆÖs world, making a serious case for the complexity of fashion as a site of social negotiation. In 18th-century Britain, taste was a moral technology. The philosopher Anthony Ashley Cooper, Third Earl of Shaftesbury, for instance, argued that beauty and goodness were intertwined, that to cultivate aesthetic discernment was to cultivate virtue. In a period when colonial trade was minting new fortunes faster than existing hierarchies could absorb them, the question of how to distinguish virtuous consumption from vulgar extravagance pressed urgently on critics and patrons alike. Taste promised to regulate luxury, refine appetite, and prove that wealth had earned its elevation. Gainsborough built a career on the visual execution of that promise.Left: Thomas Gainsborough, "Sarah Hodges, Later Lady Innes" (c. 1759), oil on canvas (photo Michael Bodycomb); right: Thomas Gainsborough, "Mary, Countess Howe" (1763ŌĆō64), oil on canvas (┬® Historic England / Bridgeman Images)A portrait of the young Sarah Hodges from 1759 depicts her with a single rosebud between her fingers and sheer fabric cascading down her sleeves, a debutante on the verge of visibility. Nearby, ŌĆ£Mary, Countess HoweŌĆØ (1763ŌĆō64) strides through a landscape in a storm of pink silk and a perfectly rendered lace apron that folds back on itself with impossible clarity, dressed to the height of fashion from her string of pearls to her heeled shoes. These women demonstrate that they belong in the company of those who, in English writer Samuel Johnson's contemporaneous definition, occupy ŌĆ£a condition above the vulgar,ŌĆØ where taste supposedly polishes wealth into virtue. Youth and status become questions of taste, calibrated in rosy cheeks and gossamer fabrics, a jeweled brooch or red shoe peeking out from a muslin skirt.This calculus of status as taste extended so far that Gainsborough would often rework certain commissions to suit the changing fads. Studies revealed that ŌĆ£Mrs. SheridanŌĆØ (1783) was repainted once her earlier shepherdess persona became pass├®, and ŌĆ£Mrs. Samuel MoodyŌĆØ (1779) revised to add her sons and remove a pearl necklace, recasting respectability in a single sweep of paint. The Frick compellingly frames these additions as part of a story of reinvention and self-fashioning.Left: Thomas Gainsborough, "Mrs. Samuel Moody and Her Sons, Samuel and Thomas" (c. 1779, reworked c. 1784), oil on canvas (┬® Dulwich Picture Gallery / Bridgeman Images); right: Thomas Gainsborough, "Mrs. Sheridan" (c. 1783, altered 1785ŌĆō87), oil on canvasBut the exhibition is at its sharpest when the moralizing framework of Georgian taste begins to fray. One pairing places ŌĆ£Mary, Duchess of MontaguŌĆØ (1768) opposite ŌĆ£Ignatius SanchoŌĆØ (1768), born into enslavement, later a composer, writer, and celebrated intellectual employed in the Montagu household. Sancho appears in a gentleman's coat and waistcoat, hand tucked into his vest. Yet GainsboroughŌĆÖs paint handling falters here; SanchoŌĆÖs skin tone slides toward the gray-green ground so that his body seems to hover, without firm footing in space. The gesture grants him dignity while exposing the limits of taste as an engine of social transformation. Fashion can reframe Sancho as ŌĆ£of fashionŌĆØ for the length of a sitting, but the structures that profit from his labor remain intact.┬ĀWhat lingers is the sense of how hard these images work to make hierarchy feel beautiful, even natural. The porcelain faces, naval uniforms, foliage, and dissolving grounds are symptoms of a world where the subject yields to the performance of good taste. The exhibition offers real pleasures in its meticulous attention to detail: silk that catches light, gold that seems to rustle, the quiet nods to shifting fashions of the social season. But its most urgent contribution lies in making visible the labor of taste itself, and the social fiction it has always sustained: the promise that style can launder wealth into moral distinction. Seen from our present, that promise looks increasingly fragile, and the paintings begin to flicker between high fantasy and indictment.Left: Thomas Gainsborough, "Mary, Duchess of Montagu" (c. 1768), oil on canvas (photo courtesy The Buccleuch Living Heritage Trust); right: Thomas Gainsborough, "Ignatius Sancho" (1768), oil on canvasInstallation view of Gainsborough: The Fashion of Portraiture Gainsborough: The Fashion of Portraiture continues at the Frick Collection (1 East 70th Street) through May 25, 2026. The exhibition was curated by Aimee Ng. 
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Reviews
                      
                      
                        Thomas Gainsborough
                      
                      
                        Frick Collection
                      
                      
                        New York

title: The Tender Work of Preserving Renee GoodŌĆÖs Memorial
description:
Feature
                A traveling photographer has decided to stay in Minneapolis to care for the hundreds of artworks, objects, an
body:
Feature
                A traveling photographer has decided to stay in Minneapolis to care for the hundreds of artworks, objects, and messages left in memory of the poet and mother.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Ryan Vizzions is archiving the objects left at the site of Renee Good's murder. (all photos by and courtesy Ryan Vizzions)Ryan Vizzions, a photojournalist from Atlanta, had already arrived in Minnesota when federal immigration agents murdered poet and mother Renee Nicole Macklin Good.┬ĀFor the last five years, the traveling photographer has been living out of his small van as he travels across the country for a photo survey exploring what it means to be American in all 50 states. He was taking photos at Lake Superior when he learned of GoodŌĆÖs killing, and drove immediately to the street where agents shot Good in her car. He arrived in time for a massive vigil held in GoodŌĆÖs memory.┬ĀNearly two months after GoodŌĆÖs murder, Vizzions is still in Minnesota, but his focus has shifted from observation to intervention. He is now the de facto archivist of GoodŌĆÖs memorial site, where mourners have left hundreds of devotional objects, short notes, and artwork in protest and in grief."I want to make sure people in the future understand what happened here,ŌĆØ Vizzions told Hyperallergic in an interview. Vizzions among Good memorial objects at his undisclosed storage siteSo far, Vizzions has photographed about 200 items and relocated fragile objects to what he described as a ŌĆ£secret locationŌĆØ in the southern part of the city. HeŌĆÖs left behind some items, including plastic signs, for the public to view. Alongside community members, Vizzions is maintaining the site, including by removing what he described as hundreds of pounds of decaying flowers.Among the items Vizzions has documented is a note signed by an employee of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), the agency driving the Trump administrationŌĆÖs escalating immigration enforcement tactics.┬ĀŌĆ£Ms. Good,ŌĆØ the message reads, ŌĆ£We will never forget you. Rest in peace and power. Your work on earth is done. Your legacy lives on.ŌĆØ┬ĀThe card, which is covered in stickers, is signed, ŌĆ£A DHS employee.ŌĆØŌĆ£That was probably the most surprising because that's somebody who is involved with the same institution that ultimately killed her,ŌĆØ Vizzions told Hyperallergic.┬ĀFlowers at Renee Good's memorial siteMourners attended a vigil for Good hours after federal agents killed her.Vizzions made the leap from outside observer to active participant in MinneapolisŌĆÖs response to GoodŌĆÖs murder after someone attempted to burn the memorial site and extreme winter conditions set in, threatening to destroy the makeshift monument. On February 18, someone poured gasoline on the memorial and lit a flame. Vizzions said that he and a group of community members watching over the site at night were able to stop the fire from spreading.While Vizzions has previously photographed political apexes, including Dakota Access Pipeline protests at Standing Rock, he said he had never before inserted himself in the communities he covers.┬ĀŌĆ£ŌĆŖAs a photojournalist, oftentimes you're divided from the community because youŌĆÖre on the outside looking in,ŌĆØ Vizzions said. ŌĆ£And I wanted to serve.ŌĆØ┬ĀVizzions was watching over the site in February when someone poured gasoline and set fire to the memorialVizzions told Hyperallergic that GoodŌĆÖs parents are aware of his project and that he is in communication with a family friend who is serving as a mediator. Ultimately, Vizzions said, he will respect the familyŌĆÖs wishes for any next steps for the collection.┬ĀHe expects that some of the items could end up in the collections of private institutions or in the archives of the Smithsonian, but noted that whatever happens next will not be his decision to make.┬ĀIn the meantime, he is photographing and digitizing items from Good's vigil so that anyone can experience them, regardless of where they live."It's ŌĆŖreally important for me to make sure that the folks who couldn't be here, and the family who couldn't come to the vigil because of everything happening, are able to access the memorial in person or online," Vizzions said. As he waits for the family to direct his next move, Vizzions is digitizing the collection of memorial objects. The photographer recalled one snow-covered note that made him cry. It read: ŌĆ£ŌĆŖWe all carry whistles now. I hope you hear them. I hope you're home. We all carry each other now. I know you're with us. I know you're home.ŌĆØ The message is a nod to activists' use of whistles to alert community members of potential immigration raids. "It was just on a small note that was tucked somewhere," Vizzons said. "But that's just one of hundreds, if not thousands, of items that people have left. It's that message and the other message that really make it feel like we have an obligation to protect these offerings that people brought to her."Vizzions collected this candle at the memorialA hat left behind at the Renee Good memorial siteA jar of unopened notes that Vizzions has stored.
                  
                      
                        Feature
                      
                      
                        Renee Nicole Good
                      
                      
                        Minneapolis

schema.headline: The Tender Work of Preserving Renee GoodŌĆÖs Memorial

title: Meet the Woman Who Made Museums More Accessible
description:
Feature
                The first head of Accessible Programs at the National Gallery of Art tells us about her path and the fut
body:
Feature
                The first head of Accessible Programs at the National Gallery of Art tells us about her path and the future of museum accessibility. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Lorena Bradford at the National Gallery of Art in 2025 (all photos Emma Cieslik/Hyperallergic)
    
        
            WASHINGTON, DC ŌĆö┬ĀLorena Bradford set out to be a speech-language pathologist, but she fell in love with art history, going on to earn a PhD in 17th-century Dutch and Flemish print-making. It would not be until 2010, however ŌĆö two years after she started working as an educator at the National Gallery of Art (NGA) in Washington, DC ŌĆö that she learned about arts accessibility, when she attended her first Leadership Exchange in Arts and Disability (LEAD) Conference.At the time, she was "confronting my own assumption that speaking equals engagement,ŌĆØ Bradford said over Zoom in January. As more autistic children were visiting galleries as part of school programming, she began asking questions about creating programs intentionally tailored for disabled students and adults. Soon after, she was hired as the first head of Accessible Programs at the NGA.┬ĀMany other museums lump together reasonable accommodations for patrons and staff along with Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) compliance into one personŌĆÖs job description, but Bradford was able to focus on tailoring programs to patronsŌĆÖ needs and wants.┬ĀŌĆ£I was a sub-department of one,ŌĆØ she joked.Lorena Bradford at the National Gallery of Art in 2025In the years that followed, she started a monthly tour in American Sign Language led by deaf guides, alongside a program for individuals with memory loss and their caregivers called Just Us, which ran from 2017 to 2024. She also established a program that brought Georgetown medical students into the gallery to learn skills that would enhance the care they provide, including observation, communication, compassion, and perspective.┬ĀBut one of her biggest endeavors was the Short Description Project. Many museums, including the National Gallery of Art, had recorded verbal descriptions of art for in-person patrons, but this was a different level of accessibility. ŌĆ£One of the things thatŌĆÖs really amazing is that you put everything online,ŌĆØ Bradford explained. ŌĆ£ThereŌĆÖs no paywall and thatŌĆÖs a huge part of accessibility, getting to access information online.ŌĆØBegun during the COVID-19 pandemic, when many museums were thinking about digital access, the Short Description Project developed a workflow for creating detailed written descriptions for all individuals encountering art online. This meant sketching out parameters for how long the descriptions could be, what should be included, what language used, what reading level they should be, and what process and training might look like, all from the ground up.┬ĀLorena Bradford at the National Gallery of Art in 2025The Trump administration, however, changed everything. While most accessible program work was and is still intrinsically connected to visitor services and protected by the ADA, ŌĆ£the strides that I and colleagues from across many, many museums were making related to diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility were curtailed or stopped,ŌĆØ Bradford said.┬ĀTwo years ago, she began undergoing cancer treatment and dropped her hours at the NGA to part-time. She officially left the museum this past July.┬ĀBut her passion for access never stopped. Now, she works as an audio describer at the Kennedy Center, writes alt text and image descriptions for Scribeley, teaches art history courses at a local community college, and curates all of the content for the LEAD conferences.┬ĀLorena Bradford at the National Gallery of Art in 2025Bradford knows that her educational and career path is unconventional. As more museum education programs come into being, ŌĆ£thereŌĆÖs not necessarily a place you can go to learn to do cultural accessibility like what weŌĆÖre doing in museums and theaters,ŌĆØ she said.She looks forward to the day ŌĆö which she believes will be sooner rather than later ŌĆö when arts accessibility will not only be a distinct, formalized educational path, but there will be ways to accredit people in arts accessibility programming, services, and coordination. ┬ĀŌĆ£[Art is] a universal language, and everyone should have access to it," Bradford said. "You donŌĆÖt have to speak a foreign language to look at a painting made in a different country, or to listen to a piece of music. As a universal language, it should be accessible to the universe.ŌĆØ
                  
                      
                        Feature
                      
                      
                        Accessibility
                      
                      
                        Museums
                      
                      
                        Washington DC
                      
                      
                        National Gallery of Art

schema.headline: Meet the Woman Who Made Museums More Accessible
